title: Letters from the Cloud Garden
synopsis: Written as letters to a departing friend, this layered story follows Nia as she tends a magical garden growing on the underside of a cloud, while uncovering the tale of her grandmother who once tended the same impossible place during a time when such wonders were hidden from the world.

chapters.title: The First Letter I Never Sent
chapters.content:
You left your favorite pen behind when you packed your rocket ship, the silver one with the star at the tip that writes in three colors if you twist it just right. I found it this morning when I was checking the cloud soil and I thought about running after your ship but the garden needed me and besides you were already past the fourth ring of Saturn by then I bet.

I'm writing to you anyway because Grandmama always said words have wings even when the person you're talking to can't hear them yet and maybe especially then. She told me stories about this garden you know, about how she used to tend it when she was young like us but different because the world was different then and people like her had to keep magical places secret or risk losing them forever.

The garden grows on the underside of Cloud Number Seven, the fat puffy one that looks like a sleeping whale if you squint from Earth's surface, but we never squinted did we because we lived right here in the garden where the roots dangle down toward the planet and the leaves catch starlight instead of sunlight. Remember how we used to lie on our backs and watch Earth spin below us, all blue and green and perfect while the cloud-soil tickled our necks and the upside-down flowers brushed our cheeks with their velvet petals?

Grandmama's stories always started the same way: "Child, let me tell you about 1963 when I was twelve years old and the world thought girls who looked like us couldn't reach the stars." Then she'd show me the old photographs hidden in the seed packets, pictures of her in a silver space suit standing right here among these same impossible plants that grow with their roots in cloud-fluff and their faces turned toward infinity.

I wish you hadn't left so sudden-like but I understand why you had to go chase that comet that only appears every seventy-seven years and I promise I'll keep the garden growing until you come back even if it takes seventy-seven years though I hope it doesn't because that would make us very old and wrinkly.

illustrations.description: A girl with long braids with colorful beads, dark brown skin, and warm kind eyes sits on fluffy cloud material writing with a silver star-tipped pen, surrounded by plants growing upside down with their roots in the clouds and faces toward space, rendered in retro 1960s space age style with chrome details, bubble-shaped elements, and warm orange metallic tones
illustrations.alt: Nia writing a letter in the cloud garden

chapters.title: What Grandmama's Pictures Showed
chapters.content:
You remember the seed vault don't you, the chrome cylinder with the round porthole window where we kept all the special seeds that only grow in impossible places like cloud-gardens and moon-meadows and the spaces between raindrops? Well today I opened it to plant the new batch of starflower seeds and there they were again, Grandmama's pictures tucked between the packets like pressed flowers but made of light and memory instead of petals.

In the first picture she's standing right where you used to stand when you were watering the comet-tail grass with that bubble-helmet watering can of yours, but in her picture the garden looks smaller somehow and she's wearing a silver suit that's all bulky and awkward not like our simple garden clothes that let us move easy through the dangling roots and hanging vines. Her face through the helmet is young and surprised like she can't quite believe she's really here above the clouds with dirt under her fingernails that isn't earthbound dirt at all.

Grandmama told me once that in 1963 people said girls with skin like ours and hair like ours weren't supposed to touch the stars weren't supposed to reach for impossible things weren't supposed to believe in gardens that grew upside down in the sky but she came anyway because somebody had to tend the plants and she could hear them calling to her in dreams. "The garden chooses its gardener," she always said, "and it doesn't care what the world thinks about who should be allowed to touch magic."

The second picture shows her hands holding a handful of cloud-soil and it's glowing just like it glows for us when we're planting new seeds or transplanting the meteor-moss to a spot with better starlight. In the picture you can see Earth far below looking so blue and small and I wonder if she felt as far from home as I feel from you now that you're zooming through space chasing that comet with its tail of ice and ancient light.

But the third picture is my favorite because it shows the garden in full bloom just like it is now with the rainbow-root trees hanging down like nature's chandeliers and the cloud-lilies opening their faces to catch cosmic dust for breakfast and there's Grandmama right in the middle of it all smiling the same smile I see in mirrors when I'm working here among the impossible growing things.

illustrations.description: A girl with long braids with colorful beads, dark brown skin, and warm kind eyes examines vintage photographs while standing next to a chrome cylindrical seed vault with a round porthole window, surrounded by hanging plants with roots in clouds and comet-tail grass, all rendered in retro 1960s space age style with optimistic futurism, chrome and orange warm metallic tones
illustrations.alt: Nia looking at her grandmother's old photographs by the seed vault

chapters.title: The Story Inside the Story
chapters.content:
You know how stories work don't you, how sometimes there's a story living inside another story like nested boxes or Russian dolls and you have to open one to find the next one hiding underneath? Well Grandmama's story is like that and today while I was pruning the nebula-berries I remembered the part she told me about why she first came to the cloud-garden and it wasn't just because the plants called to her in dreams.

She was running away you see, running from a world that kept telling her she was too small too dark too different to reach for the extraordinary things that lived beyond the ordinary sky and she climbed up and up and up through the cloud layers until she found this place where the rules were different and the only thing that mattered was whether your hands were gentle with the growing things and whether your heart was big enough to believe in impossible gardens.

"Child," she used to say while we were weeding the stardust snapdragons together, "in 1963 they wouldn't let girls who looked like us into the space program wouldn't let us touch the rockets wouldn't let us dream of walking on moon-soil or mars-dust or any soil that wasn't planted firm on Earth's surface." But here in the cloud-garden she could wear a space suit even if it was old and patched and probably borrowed from some supply closet nobody was watching and she could tend plants that grew in zero gravity and eat berries that tasted like meteor showers.

The garden became her secret space program her own personal mission to the stars where she was the commander and the scientist and the gardener all rolled into one and every day she spent here was proof that girls like her belonged among the cosmic growing things belonged in places where the horizon curved away into infinity belonged wherever their curiosity and courage could carry them.

I think about her story while I tend the same plants she tended and I wonder if my letters to you are becoming their own story, a story about friendship that stretches across space and time and the distance between here and wherever your comet is leading you through the star-scattered darkness.

illustrations.description: A girl with long braids with colorful beads, dark brown skin, and warm kind eyes tends nebula-berries and stardust snapdragons hanging from cloud roots, with vintage space suit equipment visible nearby and Earth spinning far below, rendered in retro 1960s space age style with round elements, chrome details, and warm orange metallic tones creating an optimistic futuristic atmosphere
illustrations.alt: Nia gardening while remembering her grandmother's stories

chapters.title: What the Plants Remember
chapters.content:
Did you know that plants have memories? I discovered this today when I was transplanting the aurora-ivy to a spot with better cosmic wind flow and suddenly I could feel what the garden felt all those decades ago when Grandmama first arrived with her borrowed space suit and her determined heart and her hands that shook just a little because she'd never touched magic before but she was ready to learn.

The ivy remembers her footsteps light and careful on the cloud-soil that first day when she didn't know yet which plants liked to be talked to and which ones preferred silence and she didn't know that the meteor-moss would glow brighter if you hummed while you watered it or that the comet-tail grass grows in spirals when it's happy but shoots straight up like exclamation points when it's worried about something.

The rainbow-root trees remember how she used to sit beneath their hanging branches and cry sometimes because even though she'd found this perfect impossible place where she could be exactly who she was meant to be she still missed the world below the clouds missed her family missed the chance to show everyone that girls like her could reach just as far as anyone else toward the infinite mystery of space.

But the cloud-lilies remember her laughter too especially the day she figured out that if you planted the starflower seeds during a meteor shower they'd bloom in patterns that matched the falling stars and from that day forward the garden became not just her hiding place but her laboratory her classroom her proof that wonder grows best in hands that have been told they're not supposed to touch it.

The plants remember everything and today they're whispering to me about you too about how the garden feels different since you left like something bright has gone out of the cosmic wind and even though I'm here tending and watering and weeding just like always the growing things know that part of their family is missing and they're stretching their leaves toward the path your rocket took hoping you'll follow it back home soon.

illustrations.description: A girl with long braids with colorful beads, dark brown skin, and warm kind eyes kneels among glowing aurora-ivy and rainbow-root trees hanging from clouds, with her hands touching the plants as translucent memory-images of another gardener from the past shimmer around her, all rendered in retro 1960s space age style with chrome gardening tools, bubble elements, and warm orange metallic tones
illustrations.alt: Nia connecting with the plant memories in the cloud garden

chapters.title: The Day Everything Changed
chapters.content:
You left on a Tuesday but I didn't start writing these letters until Friday because Tuesday I was too mad and Wednesday I was too sad and Thursday I was too busy trying to prove I could take care of the whole garden by myself without missing you at all which turned out to be impossible because missing you is like trying not to breathe it just happens whether you want it to or not.

But Friday I remembered what Grandmama told me about the day everything changed for her the day in 1963 when she realized she couldn't keep the cloud-garden secret forever because secrets that beautiful are meant to be shared even if sharing them is scary even if the world isn't ready even if people might not believe or understand or accept that magic can grow in hands that look like yours and mine.

She started small she said started by telling her little sister about the plants that grew upside down in cloud-soil and the berries that tasted like starlight and the way Earth looked from above like a blue marble rolling through black velvet and at first her sister thought she was making up stories but then Grandmama brought her a single starflower still glowing with cosmic pollen and her sister's eyes went wide with wonder.

One person became two became three became a small group of girls who climbed up through the cloud layers on weekend afternoons to help tend the impossible garden and learn that they belonged among the stars just as much as anyone else did and some of those girls grew up to become real astronauts and scientists and explorers because once you've touched magic with your own hands it's hard to believe anyone who tells you you're not supposed to reach for extraordinary things.

"The garden taught me," Grandmama always said, "that some secrets are meant to be kept and some secrets are meant to be doors that you open for other people so they can walk through into their own impossible places." And today while I was writing to you I realized that these letters are my way of opening a door keeping our friendship alive across all the space between here and your comet keeping the story growing even when one of the storytellers is zooming through the cosmic dark.

illustrations.description: A girl with long braids with colorful beads, dark brown skin, and warm kind eyes stands among blooming starflowers in the cloud garden, holding a glowing blossom while ghostly images of other young gardeners from the past work alongside her, rendered in retro 1960s space age style with optimistic futurism, chrome details, bubble-shaped flowers, and warm orange metallic tones
illustrations.alt: Nia holding a starflower while remembering her grandmother's expanding circle of gardeners

chapters.title: What I'm Learning About Distance
chapters.content:
Distance is a funny thing when you're living in a garden that grows upside down above the clouds because up here everything is already impossibly far from everything else but somehow that makes the far things feel closer not farther like the rules of missing someone work backwards in places where gravity doesn't know which way to pull.

I'm learning that missing you isn't just about wishing you were here to help me water the nebula-berries or laugh at the way the meteor-moss tickles when it's happy it's about all the stories I'm collecting that I want to tell you about how the garden changes a little bit every day and how I'm starting to understand why Grandmama kept coming back here even after she was old and her space suit didn't fit quite right anymore.

She came back because the garden was where she first learned she was brave enough to reach for impossible things and once you learn that about yourself you need to keep practicing it keep reminding your hands and your heart that they were made for touching magic that they belong wherever wonder grows wildest whether that's in a cloud-garden or a rocket ship or a laboratory or anywhere else that curiosity leads you.

Today I found something new growing in the spot where we used to sit together when we were planning your comet-chasing expedition a plant I've never seen before with leaves that look like tiny silver envelopes and when I touched one it opened up and inside was a message written in starlight that said "Some friendships bloom across any distance" and I think maybe the garden is learning to write letters too.

I'm learning that Grandmama's story and my story and your story are all part of the same big story about what happens when people who've been told they don't belong in magical places decide to tend those places anyway and make them bloom brighter than anyone thought possible and maybe that's why I keep writing these letters because every word is a seed I'm planting in the space between here and wherever you are.

illustrations.description: A girl with long braids with colorful beads, dark brown skin, and warm kind eyes sits cross-legged in the cloud garden examining a plant with silver envelope-shaped leaves that glow with starlight messages, surrounded by nebula-berries and meteor-moss, with a rocket trail visible in the distant sky, all rendered in retro 1960s space age style with chrome and orange warm metallic tones
illustrations.alt: Nia discovering the message plant in the cloud garden

chapters.title: The Letter I Will Send
chapters.content:
Tomorrow marks thirty days since you left chasing that comet with its seventy-seven-year schedule and its tail full of ancient ice and cosmic secrets and I've decided that tomorrow I'm going to send this letter not through normal mail that would take forever to reach you in the space between Jupiter and Saturn but through the garden's own postal system that I just figured out how to use.

You remember the rainbow-root trees don't you how their roots dangle down toward Earth like nature's telegraph wires well today I discovered that if you tie a letter to one of those roots during a meteor shower the cosmic wind will carry it anywhere in the solar system faster than light faster than rockets faster than missing someone can travel through the chambers of a heart that's learning how to be both sad and proud at the same time.

I'm proud of you for following your dream even though it meant leaving our garden and I'm proud of Grandmama for showing girls like us that we belong among the stars and I'm proud of myself for keeping the impossible plants growing and learning that some stories don't end when one of the characters zooms away into space they just change shape like clouds like comets like friendship itself.

The garden is different now but different doesn't mean worse it means new and I'm discovering corners of it I never explored when we were here together places where the starflowers grow in spirals that spell out words in languages older than Earth places where the cloud-soil hums lullabies that sound like rocket engines places where your absence has become its own kind of presence like an invisible gardener working alongside me.

So when you're riding that comet through the cosmic dark and you feel a sudden warmth like someone thinking about you with enough love to power a small star that will be this letter finding you through impossible postal routes and when you taste starlight that's sweeter than usual that will be me sharing the berries from our garden and when you see a cloud that looks like it's growing upside down you'll know you're almost ready to come home to the place where stories nest inside other stories and friendship blooms in any soil even the soil of space even the soil of time even the soil of two hearts that learned to reach for magic together.

Until your comet brings you home I'll keep tending our impossible garden and writing letters that travel on cosmic wind and believing in the same thing Grandmama believed that some places are too wonderful to keep secret and some people are too important to stop missing and some stories are too good to ever really end.

With all the love that fits in cloud-soil and starlight,
Your friend in the garden that grows upside down

illustrations.description: A girl with long braids with colorful beads, dark brown skin, and warm kind eyes ties a glowing letter to the dangling roots of rainbow-root trees during a meteor shower, with cosmic wind swirling around her and a comet visible in the distance, rendered in retro 1960s space age style with chrome details, optimistic futurism, and warm orange metallic tones against a vintage space poster background
illustrations.alt: Nia sending her letter through the garden's cosmic postal system

coverPrompt.description: A girl with long braids adorned with colorful beads, dark brown skin, and warm kind eyes tends to glowing plants hanging upside down from fluffy white clouds, with a retro 1960s space age aesthetic featuring chrome gardening tools, bubble-shaped planters, and warm metallic orange tones against a vintage space poster background
coverPrompt.alt: Nia gardening on the underside of clouds in retro futuristic style

questions.question: Where does the cloud garden grow?
choices: On top of Cloud Number Seven
On the underside of Cloud Number Seven
questions.explanation: The garden grows on the underside of Cloud Number Seven, with roots dangling down toward Earth and plants growing upside down.

questions.question: What did Nia's grandmother do in 1963?
choices: Tended the cloud garden when girls like her weren't supposed to reach for the stars
questions.explanation: Grandmama tended the cloud garden in 1963 during a time when people said girls with skin like hers weren't supposed to touch the stars or believe in magical places.

questions.question: Why is Nia writing letters to her friend?
choices: Her friend left to chase a comet
Her friend moved to another planet
questions.explanation: Nia's friend left to chase a comet that only appears every seventy-seven years, and Nia is writing letters to stay connected across the distance.

questions.question: What special thing do the plants in the garden have?
questions.explanation: Nia discovers that the plants have memories and can remember all the gardeners who have tended them over the years.

questions.question: How does Nia plan to send her letter?
choices: By tying it to the rainbow-root trees during a meteor shower
questions.explanation: Nia discovers the garden's postal system where letters tied to rainbow-root trees during meteor showers are carried by cosmic wind anywhere in the solar system.

questions.question: What did Grandmama's grandmother teach her about secrets?
choices: All secrets should be kept forever
Some secrets are meant to be kept and some are meant to be doors for other people
questions.explanation: Grandmama learned that some secrets are meant to be kept while others are meant to be doors that you open for other people so they can find their own impossible places.

questions.question: What grows in the spot where Nia and her friend used to sit?
choices: A plant with leaves like silver envelopes that contain starlight messages
questions.explanation: A new plant grows there with leaves that look like tiny silver envelopes, and when Nia touches one it opens to reveal a message written in starlight.

pages.text: The garden grows on the _____ of Cloud Number Seven, where the _____ dangle down toward Earth and the leaves catch _____ instead of sunlight.
In 1963, Grandmama came to the _____ garden because she could hear the _____ calling to her in dreams, even though people said girls like her weren't supposed to touch the _____.
